content.text: Stakeholder sign-off for a GenAI feature is not a single approval event — it is a distributed set of decisions, each owned by a different role, each with explicit criteria that determine whether the decision can be granted. A RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) makes this ownership explicit. Without a RACI, the most common failure mode is that no one blocks a bad decision because everyone assumed someone else was accountable.
Why GenAI Sign-Off Needs a RACI

Standard product sign-off often involves a product owner and an engineering lead. GenAI features require additional sign-off from AI Safety, Legal, and (for the design artifact) the designer. More importantly, some decisions can only be blocked by specific roles: the AI Safety Lead is the only person who can unblock Gate 2, regardless of business pressure; the Legal/Compliance officer is the only person who can unblock Gate 3. The RACI makes these veto rights explicit.
: when every decision has exactly one Accountable owner, 'I thought someone else was checking that' is not possible
: in GenAI, safety and legal reviewers have veto rights that cannot be overridden by product or executive pressure — the RACI records this
: consulted roles must be heard before a decision is made; informed roles are notified after — conflating the two leads to either slow decisions or ignored risks
: decisions on different rows of the RACI can be pursued concurrently if their columns are different — the RACI makes this visible
The Accountable role is the one who must answer if the decision turns out to be wrong. There can be exactly one Accountable per decision — not zero, not two.
Each decision in the RACI has explicit criteria that determine whether the sign-off can be granted. These criteria must be documented — verbal assurances do not count.
All user personas in PRD have at least one job story
PRD Section 1 job stories list — one per persona
Job stories cover happy path, correction, and failure
At least 3 job stories per flow (one per path type)
Behavior spec task scope section completed
Out-of-scope list reviewed and signed off by both
All 6 elements of behavior spec authored
BehaviorSpec.persona, task_scope, output_format, confidence, refusal_policy, citation all non-null
prompts/v<N>/system_prompt.txt exists with version tag in header
ScriptReplayResult: all turns passed for happy path and fallback scripts
The AI Safety Lead is both Accountable and Responsible for Gate 2. No other role can grant safety approval.
FMEA completed with no unresolved RPN > 200
FMEAWorkshopOutput attached; all high-RPN items have mitigations
Refusal policy documented for all identified triggers
Guardrail spec covers input and output filtering
Guardrail spec section in PRD with input+output filter chain
HITL rules defined for all irreversible actions
HITLRule list in PRD Section 5; one rule per identified irreversible action
EvalScenario with ScenarioType.VULNERABLE in eval suite
Escalation Protocol When a Gate Is Blocked
A blocked gate cannot be overridden by a product manager or an executive sponsor. The escalation protocol defines what happens when a gate is blocked and the team believes the block is incorrect.
: the team files a formal written disagreement with the gate owner, stating the specific criterion they believe is incorrectly applied
Peer review of the gate criterion
: a second reviewer from the same function (a second AI Safety reviewer, a second legal counsel) evaluates the blocked criterion independently
: if the peer review does not resolve the disagreement, the relevant VP or Chief AI Officer mediates — with the gate owner's analysis and the challenger's counter-analysis both presented

: in rare cases, the CEO or board can override a legal block with documented indemnity accepted. A safety block can never be overridden by business priority alone — this is a design principle, not a policy
The escalation protocol must be documented in the team's operating agreement before the first GenAI feature is started — not negotiated ad-hoc when a gate is blocked under launch pressure.

Sign-off state is tracked in the handoff package alongside the traceability matrix. Each decision in the RACI has a corresponding SignOffDecision object. The handoff package cannot be frozen until all required decisions are APPROVED.
Product owner signs off on safety gate
RACI not enforced — any sign-off accepted
Project management tool only accepts decision from the Accountable role (verified by email/SSO)
Two Accountable owners on one decision
RACI not reviewed before sprint begins
RACI review is agenda item at sprint kickoff; each row must have exactly one Accountable
Consulted role not engaged before decision
Email sent to I role instead of C role
Decision workflow: C roles receive form to complete before A role can mark decision as made
Gate blocked but engineering proceeds anyway
No enforcement mechanism; blocked state treated as advisory
CI/CD pipeline checks sign-off state; deployment blocked if any required decision is not APPROVED
Handoff package only accepts written decision with timestamp; verbal approval has no legal standing
RACI not updated after scope change
Scope change protocol requires RACI review as part of the change assessment

Not every decision in the RACI must wait for the one before it. Decisions that involve different Accountable roles and draw on different evidence sets can run in parallel, which significantly shortens the requirements phase timeline. Understanding which decisions can parallelize is a practical project management skill.
Behavior spec cannot be authored until scope is agreed
Safety reviews prompt and FMEA; Legal reviews data and regulatory mapping — independent evidence
Partially — design can start before Gate 2 closes
Design sign-off cannot freeze until safety-relevant UI checks are confirmed by safety reviewer
QA can define eval gate criteria while Safety reviews the FMEA
Legal reviews data consent; QA reviews performance thresholds — no dependency
Gate 4 approval must precede handoff freeze — it confirms all prior decisions are valid
The RACI translates to a specific meeting cadence during the requirements and design phase. Each sign-off decision maps to a meeting or async review with defined inputs, outputs, and SLAs.
5 business days from behavior spec submission
7 business days from safety gate submission
3 business days per round; max 3 rounds
2 business days after all prior gates APPROVED

SLAs are commitments, not aspirations. If a sign-off gate SLA is missed, the PM escalates to the Accountable role's manager — not to the team. The Accountable role is accountable for the timeline, not just the decision quality.

Occasionally a reviewer approves a decision with conditions: 'approved, provided that X is implemented before launch'. Conditional sign-offs are common in safety and legal review. They must be tracked as a separate artifact with their own resolution criteria — they are not the same as a full APPROVED decision.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class Condition:
    condition_id: str
    description: str           # what must be true before the condition is cleared
    resolution_evidence: str = ""  # what was done to satisfy this condition
    cleared: bool = False
    cleared_by: str = ""
    cleared_at: Optional[str] = None  # ISO-8601


@dataclass
class ConditionalSignOff(SignOffDecision):
    """A sign-off that is approved subject to conditions being met."""
    conditions: list[Condition] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def is_unconditionally_approved(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_approved and all(c.cleared for c in self.conditions)

    def uncleared_conditions(self) -> list[Condition]:
        return [c for c in self.conditions if not c.cleared]

    def clear_condition(
        self,
        condition_id: str,
        evidence: str,
        cleared_by: str,
        cleared_at: str,
    ) -> None:
        for condition in self.conditions:
            if condition.condition_id == condition_id:
                condition.resolution_evidence = evidence
                condition.cleared = True
                condition.cleared_by = cleared_by
                condition.cleared_at = cleared_at
                return
        raise ValueError(f"Condition {condition_id} not found")


# Example: Safety gate approved with conditions
safety_sign_off = ConditionalSignOff(
    decision_id="SAFETY-2025-07-01",
    decision_type="SAFETY",
    accountable_role="AI Safety Lead",
    responsible_role="AI Safety Lead",
    criteria_met=["FMEA complete", "Refusal policy present", "Guardrails defined"],
    criteria_failed=[],
    status="APPROVED",
    signed_off_at="2025-07-01T14:00:00Z",
    conditions=[
        Condition(
            condition_id="C1",
            description="Vulnerable-user eval scenario must be added before beta launch",
        ),
        Condition(
            condition_id="C2",
            description="HITL SLA (max 2 min human response time) must be verified in load test",
        ),
    ],
)

# Gate 4 checks for unconditional approval
ok = safety_sign_off.is_unconditionally_approved  # False — conditions not yet cleared
uncleared = safety_sign_off.uncleared_conditions()  # [C1, C2]

A ConditionalSignOff is treated as PENDING for Gate 4 purposes until all conditions are cleared. The PM tracks condition status in the handoff package and notifies the Accountable role when each condition is satisfied for final clearance.
Documenting RACI Decisions for Regulatory Audit

For GenAI features that fall under the EU AI Act (High-Risk classification) or other regulated domains (HIPAA, FINRA), the sign-off decisions are not just project management artifacts — they are legal evidence. Regulators can request documentation of who approved what and on what basis. The sign-off tracker must produce an audit-ready report.

Stakeholder sign-off for a GenAI feature is a set of discrete decisions, each with a defined Accountable owner, explicit criteria, and evidence requirements. The RACI matrix makes ownership, veto rights, and consultation requirements explicit. The AI Safety Lead is the sole Accountable for Gate 2 — no other role can override a safety block. The Legal/Compliance officer is the sole Accountable for Gate 3. Sign-off state is tracked in the handoff package as SignOffDecision objects and gates both Gate 4 eligibility and the final handoff package freeze.
: multiple accountability is no accountability — the RACI must be enforced, not advisory
: AI Safety and Legal blocks cannot be overridden by product or executive pressure — the escalation protocol handles genuine disagreements
: consulted roles must provide input before the decision is made; informed roles are notified after — mixing them causes either delay or missed risks
: SignOffTracker records each decision with criteria met/failed, status, timestamp, and accountable role — the full audit trail lives in the handoff package
: Final approval cannot be granted until SCOPE, BEHAVIOR_SPEC, SAFETY, LEGAL, and DESIGN are all APPROVED
